focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 7

Cora crossed the kitchen when the screen door rasped and caught it on the hook before the sound could fret her worse. The dishrag was twisted hard between both hands. August's note lay under the sugar jar where she had shoved it after breakfast. Mr. Walter Knapp of the resettlement office would call at eleven. Kindly remain on the premises. By then she had already blacked the stove as well as cheap polish allowed, beaten and rehung the flour-sack curtains, turned the cups so the worst chips faced the wall, and set the enamel basin mouth-down to hide the rust along the rim. None of it improved the house. It only showed that she had spent the morning trying.

Kindly had been the word that raised the heat in her, more even than office. There was nowhere else for her to go at eleven on a Wednesday except the farm she was being asked to watch die on schedule.

Outside, dust moved low over the yard in nervous little drifts that gathered at the porch steps and broke apart. Out by the pump Dell had the hood off the truck again, though truck was grander than what sat there: a Ford with one fender tied on by wire and a temperament meaner than a kicked mule. He leaned in with a wrench in his fist, shirt stuck dark between his shoulders, wrists all bone and tendon. He had come in late the night before with damp earth on his hands and some tight silence he had not yet spent, and August's note had fallen across the morning before she could force the rest of it out of him. When he straightened he looked older from the back than he ever did from the front.

"You want the hood down before he gets here?" he called.

"Leave it."

He wiped his forearm over his face and left a gray streak across his cheek. "He'll look at it and write down we're half junkyard."

"Then let him wr...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...saw any pair of eyes in any house where dust settled after a storm and nobody could stop the weather from keeping receipts. The thought was not grand. It was domestic, and that made it worse.

"He didn't," Cora said.

"No." Thea swallowed. Her voice came back with effort. "But if I stay, that's part of what staying is."

Cora turned then. Thea stood naked except for the one stocking still around her ankle, hair down, letter on the washstand behind her, the mark of Cora's mouth still visible at her collarbone. There was no decent lie left in the room. "Yes," Cora said.

Thea measured the basin, the wall, the door, the letter, the bed too near the wall and the town beyond it. Then she reached back without looking, took the folded Lincoln letter from beside the lamp, and slid it under Cora's brush instead of into her dress.

"I'll wire them Monday for another day," she said. "I'm not promising you harvest in the dark. But I'm not walking into Lincoln Tuesday pretending this room doesn't change anything. If you still mean it when the sun's up, say it then. Until Lincoln answers, I'm here."

She took the cloth from the gray water, scrubbed the inside of the basin once, hard, and turned it mouth-down on the washstand. A thread of water slid from under the rim and darkened the wood beside the lamp. Only then did she come back to the bed and slide in under the coverlet, one cold foot against Cora's shin, one hand flat on her belly. On the far side of the wall Dell's rescued clock kept its crooked tick. Under the upturned basin, a trapped drop struck wood, then another, then stopped. Cora did not sleep at once. She lay under Thea's hand listening to that crooked tick and thinking of breakfast, of daylight, of having to say the same hard thing with Dell at the table and mean it.
